title: Student Conduct, Discipline, and Restorative Practices

content.text: Discipline at Eduversal Partner Schools serves an educational rather than purely punitive purpose. The ultimate objective of education is self-governance—developing not only the ability to understand right from wrong but also the capacity to exercise that understanding in making ethical choices. All persons participating in the school community are subject to its rules and regulations; joining this community carries the corresponding obligation to follow its standards. When students err, the goal of discipline is to guide them back to understanding what is appropriate and to help them act accordingly. The school is granted considerable discretion in developing discipline criteria and policy, conditioned on each student's acceptance of the obligation to abide by the lawful rules of the school community. The intent of this policy is to enable school officials to protect the educational environment by setting clear behavioral expectations and consequences, and by removing distractions from that environment when necessary.
content.title: 22.1.1 Educational Purpose of Discipline

content.text: The following principles guide all disciplinary decisions and interventions: Restorative Focus: The school prioritizes repairing harm, restoring relationships, and reintegrating students into the community over punitive measures alone. While consequences are necessary, the primary aim is to help students learn from mistakes and develop stronger character. Due Process: Every student has the right to be heard, to understand allegations against them, and to present their perspective before consequences are determined. No student shall be punished without opportunity to respond to concerns. This principle aligns with Indonesian child protection requirements ensuring fair treatment of minors. Proportionality: Consequences must match the severity of the behavior, considering context, intent, student history, developmental stage, and any mitigating or aggravating circumstances. A first-time minor offense should not receive the same consequence as a repeated serious violation. Consistency: Behavioral expectations and consequences apply equitably across all students regardless of academic standing, family background, financial status, or social connections. The school must avoid any appearance of favoritism or discrimination in disciplinary matters. Cultural Sensitivity: The school respects diverse cultural backgrounds while maintaining universal standards of respect and safety. For Islamic boarding schools, discipline integrates Islamic values and character development principles. For all schools, EAL learners receive appropriate support to understand expectations, and cultural differences in communication styles are considered during investigations. Student Voice: Students are involved in developing classroom expectations and resolving conflicts through peer mediation and restorative conversations. Student input is valued in policy development and review processes. Positive Reinforcement: The school emphasizes recognition and celebration of positive behavior alongside addressing misconduct. A comprehensive approach to behavior management includes both consequences for inappropriate behavior and acknowledgment of exemplary conduct. Transparency: All rules, expectations, and consequences are clearly communicated to students and parents in accessible language (English and Bahasa Indonesia). There should be no surprises regarding what behavior is expected or what consequences may result from violations.
content.title: 22.1.2 Core Principles

content.text: The discipline framework supports the development of Schoolwide Learner Outcomes (SLOs), particularly:

items: Ethical Leadership: Students demonstrate integrity, take responsibility for their actions, and model positive behavior for peers.
Global Competence: Students show respect for diverse perspectives and resolve conflicts through dialogue and understanding.
Communication: Students express concerns appropriately and participate constructively in conflict resolution.
Wellbeing: Students develop self-regulation skills and contribute to a positive school climate.
content.title: 22.1.3 Alignment with School Values and Learning Outcomes

content.text: The framework also integrates Profil Pelajar Pancasila (P8) character competencies:

items: Beriman, Bertakwa kepada Tuhan YME, dan Berakhlak Mulia: Acting with moral integrity guided by faith and ethical principles.
Gotong Royong: Collaborating to maintain a positive community and supporting peers in making good choices.
Mandiri: Taking personal responsibility for behavior and self-regulation.
Bernalar Kritis: Reflecting on actions and their consequences; making thoughtful decisions.
Berkebinekaan Global: Respecting diversity and treating all community members with dignity.

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 1.3 for detailed Schoolwide Learner Outcomes; Section 2.4 for Profil Pelajar Pancasila integration requirements.

Student conduct and discipline intersect with safeguarding in several important ways. Peer-on-peer harm (bullying, harassment, abuse between students) is addressed through both the behavior policy and safeguarding procedures. When a discipline matter involves potential safeguarding concerns, the Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) must be informed and may take the lead in managing the situation. Discipline responses must never compromise child protection; the safety and wellbeing of all students takes precedence. Cross-Reference: See Section 9 for comprehensive safeguarding policies and reporting procedures.
content.title: 22.1.4 Relationship to Safeguarding

content.text: The Student Code of Conduct applies to all students enrolled in Eduversal Partner Schools from Early Years through A-Level. The Code governs behavior in the following contexts:

items: During school hours on school premises
Before and after school while on campus
During school-sponsored activities, whether on or off campus
On school transportation (buses, vehicles arranged by school)
During school trips, excursions, and residential programs
At school-related events (sports competitions, performances, ceremonies)
In any context where behavior affects the school community or brings the school into disrepute
Online interactions and social media when related to school or affecting school community members
For boarding students, in dormitories and residential facilities at all times
content.title: 22.2.1 Scope and Application

content.text: The following matrix outlines expected behaviors organized by domain, aligned with SLOs and P8 competencies: Domain: Respect for Self Expected Behaviors: Maintain personal dignity and positive self-image; take pride in appearance and presentation; demonstrate integrity and honesty in all academic work and interactions; make healthy choices; develop self-discipline and self-regulation skills. Practical Examples: Wearing proper uniform correctly; maintaining personal hygiene; completing own work without cheating; being punctual; taking care of personal belongings; making positive choices about health and safety. Domain: Respect for Others Expected Behaviors: Treat all community members (students, teachers, staff, visitors) with kindness and courtesy; practice active listening; value diverse perspectives and backgrounds; use appropriate language; maintain appropriate physical boundaries; support and encourage peers. Practical Examples: Using polite language (please, thank you, excuse me); not interrupting when others speak; including classmates in activities; avoiding gossip and rumors; respecting personal space; offering help to those in need; celebrating others' successes. Domain: Respect for Learning Expected Behaviors: Come to class prepared with required materials; participate actively in lessons; complete assignments diligently and on time; support the learning of others; minimize disruptions; engage with feedback constructively; demonstrate intellectual honesty. Practical Examples: Bringing textbooks, notebooks, and supplies; contributing to class discussions; asking questions when confused; meeting deadlines; keeping noise levels appropriate; not distracting classmates; citing sources properly. Domain: Respect for Environment Expected Behaviors: Care for school property and facilities; maintain cleanliness in classrooms, common areas, and grounds; practice environmental responsibility; use resources appropriately; report damage or maintenance needs. Practical Examples: Disposing of waste properly (recycling where available); keeping desks and lockers organized; not writing on walls or furniture; turning off lights and fans when leaving rooms; treating books and equipment carefully; reporting broken items. Domain: Safety and Security Expected Behaviors: Follow all safety protocols and procedures; report safety concerns promptly; make safe choices in physical activities; look out for peer welfare; cooperate with safety drills; avoid bringing dangerous items to school. Practical Examples: Following laboratory safety rules; participating seriously in fire and earthquake drills; reporting hazards to teachers; using playground equipment safely; not engaging in dangerous games or challenges; keeping emergency exits clear. Domain: Digital Citizenship Expected Behaviors: Use technology responsibly and ethically; follow Acceptable Use Policy; maintain appropriate online behavior; protect personal and others' privacy; report cyberbullying or inappropriate content; use AI tools only as permitted. Practical Examples: Not sharing passwords; avoiding inappropriate websites; being respectful in online communications; not posting embarrassing photos of others; reporting online concerns to adults; acknowledging AI assistance when used.
content.title: 22.2.2 Behavioral Expectations Matrix

content.text: While core values remain consistent, behavioral expectations are developmentally appropriate: Early Years and Primary (K-6): Focus on basic social skills, following instructions, kindness to others, taking care of belongings, and learning classroom routines. Expectations are simple, concrete, and frequently reinforced. Adults take primary responsibility for teaching and modeling expected behaviors. Lower Secondary (Years 7-8): Increased expectations for self-management, organization, and taking responsibility. Students begin developing conflict resolution skills and understanding consequences of choices. Peer relationships become more significant, requiring attention to social dynamics. Upper Secondary IGCSE (Years 9-10): Students expected to demonstrate greater independence, leadership, and accountability. Academic integrity expectations increase. Students should model positive behavior for younger students and contribute to school culture. A-Level (Years 11-12): Near-adult expectations for self-regulation, integrity, and leadership. Students serve as role models and may take formal leadership positions. Preparation for university-level expectations of independence and responsibility.
content.title: 22.2.3 Age-Appropriate Expectations

content.text: Schools ensure all community members understand behavioral expectations through multiple channels: Student and Parent Handbook: The complete Code of Conduct is published in the Student and Parent Handbook, available in both English and Bahasa Indonesia. Hard copies are distributed at enrollment; digital versions are available on the school website and EduNav parent portal. Enrollment Agreement: Parents and students sign an acknowledgment confirming they have received, read, and agree to follow the Code of Conduct as a condition of enrollment. Orientation Programs: Dedicated sessions during new student orientation explain expectations, consequences, and support systems. Beginning-of-year assemblies reinforce expectations for returning students. Classroom Agreements: Teachers develop class-specific expectations collaboratively with students during the first week of school. These agreements align with school-wide standards while addressing specific classroom contexts. Classroom agreements are posted visibly and referenced regularly. Visual Reminders: Age-appropriate posters, displays, and signage throughout the school reinforce key expectations. Common areas feature school values and behavioral expectations in student-friendly language. Regular Reinforcement: Teachers incorporate behavioral expectations into daily routines, recognizing positive behavior and addressing concerns promptly. Assemblies periodically address behavior themes. Parent Communication: Regular newsletters, parent meetings, and parent-teacher conferences include discussion of behavioral expectations and student conduct. Parents receive annual reminders about the Code of Conduct.
content.title: 22.2.4 Communication of Expectations

subsections.title: Classification of Behaviors and Progressive Consequences

content.text: Behaviors are classified into four levels based on severity, impact on others, and potential for harm. This progressive system ensures proportionate responses while providing clear escalation pathways. Classification considers:

items: Nature and severity of the behavior
Impact on individuals and the community
Intent (deliberate vs. accidental)
Frequency (first offense vs. repeated pattern)
Context and circumstances
Student's age and developmental level
Any relevant special educational needs or circumstances
content.title: 22.3.1 Behavior Classification Framework

content.text: Definition: Behaviors that disrupt the teaching and learning process or class order but do not cause significant harm to others or property. These are typically handled immediately by the witnessing teacher or staff member. If the same offense occurs three times, it escalates to Level 2. Examples of Level 1 Behaviors:

items: Late arrival to class (up to 10 minutes)
Not bringing required learning materials
Minor uniform violations (untucked shirt, missing tie)
Off-task behavior, excessive chatting during lessons
Incomplete or late homework
Eating or drinking in class without permission
Minor disruptions (tapping, humming, minor horseplay)
Sleeping in class
Failure to follow classroom procedures
Not participating in compulsory activities
Disposing of trash improperly
Using electronic devices without permission (first offense)
content.title: 22.3.2 Level 1: Minor Infractions (Classroom-Managed)

content.text: Possible Responses to Level 1:

items: Verbal reminder or redirection
Non-verbal cue (proximity, eye contact, gesture)
Private conversation with student
Seat change
Brief written reflection
Loss of minor privilege (e.g., choice of activity)
Break-time task or brief duty
Teacher contact with parent (if pattern emerges)
Recording in teacher notes

content.text: Handling Authority: Classroom teacher or witnessing staff member Documentation: Teachers maintain their own records; formal incident report not required unless pattern emerges (3+ occurrences) Timeline: Addressed immediately during or after class

Definition: Actions that disrupt the learning environment more broadly, represent repeated Level 1 behaviors, or demonstrate disrespect but do not endanger others' safety. These require intervention by the School Culture (SC) Coordinator and typically involve homeroom teacher and parent communication. Examples of Level 2 Behaviors:

items: Repeated Level 1 offenses (3+ occurrences)
Multiple different Level 1 offenses in short period
Failure to complete Level 1 consequences
Unauthorized mobile phone use during class
Missing class without permission (truancy)
Leaving school grounds without authorization
Disrespectful language or attitude toward staff
Inappropriate language (profanity, vulgar speech)
Minor vandalism (graffiti, minor property damage)
Rough play that could lead to injury
Academic dishonesty (first offense, minor)
Failure to follow exam regulations (minor)
Spreading rumors or gossip causing distress
Accessing inappropriate content on school devices
Violating dormitory curfew or schedule (boarding schools)
content.title: 22.3.3 Level 2: Moderate Infractions (School Culture Coordinator-Managed)

content.text: Possible Responses to Level 2:

items: Meeting with SC Coordinator
Confiscation of property (devices, inappropriate items)
After-school detention (30-60 minutes)
Saturday detention for boarding schools
Written parent notification
Parent conference (phone or in-person)
Behavior contract with specific targets
Restorative conversation with affected parties
Community service within school
Temporary loss of privileges (extracurricular participation, special events)
Cannot leave campus early (boarding schools)
Written reflection and apology
All Level 1 responses may also apply

content.text: Handling Authority: SC Coordinator in consultation with Homeroom Teacher Documentation: Formal Incident Report filed in EduNav; copy to homeroom teacher; parent notification documented Timeline: Investigation and response within 48 hours

Definition: Actions against people or property that cause or could cause harm, repeated Level 2 behaviors, or serious violations of school values. These require formal disciplinary procedures, written warning letters, and mandatory parent involvement. While serious, these behaviors may still be addressed without long-term exclusion if appropriate interventions are implemented. Examples of Level 3 Behaviors:

items: Repeated Level 2 offenses
Accumulated multiple Level 2 offenses
Physical altercation (fighting) without serious injury
Bullying or harassment (verbal, social, physical)
Intimidation or threats toward others
Theft or extortion of minor value
Significant property damage (intentional or reckless)
Repeated academic dishonesty
Forgery of documents, signatures, or records
Possession of inappropriate materials (pornography, etc.)
Gambling on school premises
Defiance of authority (refusing direct instruction from staff)
Leaving campus without permission during school hours
Inappropriate physical contact or boundary violations
Recording others without consent
Cyberbullying or online harassment
Bringing school into disrepute through public behavior
Dating relationships violating school policy (for applicable schools)
content.title: 22.3.4 Level 3: Serious Infractions (Principal-Managed)

content.text: Possible Responses to Level 3:

items: First or Second Warning Letter
In-school suspension (1-3 days)
Out-of-school suspension (1-3 days)
Mandatory parent conference with Principal
Restorative conference with victim(s)
Formal apology (written and/or verbal)
Restitution for damages or theft
Behavioral intervention plan
Mandatory counseling sessions
Removal from leadership positions
Suspension from extracurricular activities
Report to Cambridge (for exam-related misconduct)
Cannot return home on weekends (boarding schools, temporary)
All Level 1-2 responses may also apply

content.text: Handling Authority: Principal with SC Coordinator input and recommendations Documentation: Formal investigation report; Warning Letter with parent acknowledgment; all records maintained in EduNav and student file Timeline: Investigation completed within 5 school days; decision communicated in writing

Definition: Behaviors that endanger others' safety, involve illegal activities, cause serious harm, or fundamentally violate the school's values. These may result in long-term suspension or expulsion and potentially involve law enforcement. Level 4 behaviors are often also safeguarding concerns requiring DSL involvement. Examples of Level 4 Behaviors:

items: Repeated Level 3 offenses despite interventions
Possession, use, or distribution of illegal drugs or alcohol
Possession of weapons or dangerous items
Serious physical assault causing injury
Severe bullying or harassment
Sexual harassment or assault
Threats of serious violence
Arson or fire-setting
Major theft or extortion
Serious vandalism causing significant damage
Criminal activity on or off campus
Bringing drugs, alcohol, or weapons to school
Distributing illegal substances
Gang-related activity
Serious violations of Indonesian law
Actions endangering the safety of the school community
Examination malpractice (serious, such as stealing papers)
Actions causing serious reputational damage to school
content.title: 22.3.5 Level 4: Severe Infractions (Discipline Committee/Board-Managed)

content.text: Possible Responses to Level 4:

items: Third Warning Letter
Long-term suspension (4-10 days or more)
Expulsion recommendation to Yayasan Board
Police notification and involvement
Report to relevant authorities
Mandatory external counseling or intervention
DSL notification and safeguarding procedures (if applicable)
Report to Cambridge Assessment (for exam malpractice)
Withdrawal of Cambridge examination candidacy
All Level 1-3 responses may also apply

content.text: Handling Authority: Discipline Committee (for recommendation); Yayasan Board (for expulsion decision) Documentation: Comprehensive investigation file; Discipline Committee minutes; Yayasan Board decision record; all communications documented Timeline: Emergency response as needed; formal investigation within 5 school days; Discipline Committee within 5 additional days; Yayasan Board decision within 10 school days of Committee recommendation

Warning letters are formal documentation of serious misconduct, prepared by the School Culture Coordinator and issued with Principal approval. They serve as official record and escalation pathway: First Warning Letter (Surat Peringatan Pertama/SP-1): Issued for Level 3 violations. The letter documents the incident, identifies violated policies, outlines expected behavior changes, and specifies consequences for further violations. Parents must acknowledge receipt in writing. The letter remains in student file for the academic year. Second Warning Letter (Surat Peringatan Kedua/SP-2): Issued for subsequent Level 3 violations or continued patterns of concern after SP-1. Includes behavior contract with specific measurable targets and timeline. Mandatory parent conference with Principal required. The student may be placed on behavioral probation with defined conditions. Third Warning Letter (Surat Peringatan Ketiga/SP-3): Issued for Level 4 violations or continued serious misconduct after SP-2. Initiates expulsion consideration process. Student may be immediately suspended pending Discipline Committee review. Parents formally notified of potential expulsion. Important Notes on Warning Letters:

items: In cases of severe Level 4 violations, warning letters may be bypassed to proceed directly to suspension or expulsion consideration.
Second and third warning letters may be issued for repeated Level 3 violations without necessarily following the sequence (i.e., accumulation of serious incidents may warrant SP-2 or SP-3 directly).
Warning letters from previous academic years may be considered in decisions but do not automatically carry forward.
Warning letters are confidential documents shared only with relevant school personnel and the student's family.
content.title: 22.3.6 Warning Letter System

content.text: When determining the appropriate classification and consequence, decision-makers consider: Mitigating Factors (may reduce severity of response):

items: First offense with clean prior record
Genuine remorse and acceptance of responsibility
Full cooperation with investigation
Voluntary disclosure before discovery
Circumstances suggesting provocation
Significant personal stressors or challenges
Developmental or special educational needs affecting judgment
Cultural misunderstanding (particularly for new students or EAL learners)
Peer pressure with evidence of reluctant participation
content.title: 22.3.7 Factors Affecting Classification and Consequences

content.text: Aggravating Factors (may increase severity of response):

items: Premeditation or planning
Leadership role in group misconduct
Targeting vulnerable individuals
Repeat offense despite prior intervention
Lack of remorse or acceptance of responsibility
Attempt to conceal evidence or deceive investigators
Involvement of others or encouraging their participation
Violation of trust position (student leader, etc.)
Significant harm caused to victim(s)
Actions during examinations or official events

content.text: All disciplinary procedures adhere to the following principles:

items: Presumption of Innocence: Students are presumed innocent until evidence supports a finding of responsibility.
Right to Be Heard: Students have the opportunity to share their perspective before consequences are determined.
Confidentiality: Discipline matters are handled confidentially; information shared only with those who need to know.
Timeliness: Investigations and decisions are completed as promptly as possible to minimize anxiety and disruption.
Documentation: All significant actions are documented in writing and maintained securely.
Support: Students receive appropriate support throughout the process, including access to counseling if needed.
content.title: 22.4.1 General Principles of Procedure

content.text: Step 1: Initial Report The teacher or staff member who witnesses or becomes aware of an incident reports to the SC Coordinator using the standardized Incident Report Form. For minor incidents (Level 1), teachers may handle directly and record in their own notes. For Level 2 and above, formal report should be submitted within 24 hours of the incident or discovery. The initial report includes:

items: Date, time, and location of incident
Description of what occurred (factual, not interpretive)
Names of students involved
Names of witnesses
Any immediate actions taken
Any physical evidence available
content.title: 22.4.2 Investigation Process

content.text: Step 2: Initial Assessment The SC Coordinator reviews the report and conducts initial assessment to determine:

items: Preliminary classification level
Urgency of response required
Whether safeguarding procedures need to be activated
Whether student(s) should be separated from classes during investigation
Who needs to be notified (Principal, DSL, parents)

content.text: Step 3: Evidence Collection The SC Coordinator (or Principal for Level 3-4) gathers evidence:

items: Written statements from all witnesses (students and staff)
Review of relevant documentation (attendance records, prior incidents, etc.)
Collection of physical evidence if applicable
Review of CCTV footage if relevant and available
Screenshots of digital communications if cyberbullying involved
Any other relevant information

content.text: Witness statements should be taken individually to avoid collaboration. For younger students, statements may be verbal with adult recording the information. Step 4: Student Interview The student alleged to have committed the offense is interviewed:

items: Interview conducted in private, calm setting
Student informed of allegations and given opportunity to respond
Student may have support person present (homeroom teacher, counselor)
For serious matters (Level 3-4) or younger students, parents may be present
Student's account is documented in writing
Student asked to provide any witnesses or evidence in their favor

content.text: Step 5: Parent Notification Parents are informed of the investigation:

items: For Level 1: At teacher discretion, usually if pattern emerges
For Level 2: Phone call followed by written communication (email/letter)
For Level 3-4: Immediate phone call, formal written notification, invitation to meeting

content.text: Parent notification includes:

items: Nature of the concern being investigated
Process being followed
Opportunity for parent input
Information about support available to student
Timeline for decision

content.text: Step 6: Analysis and Recommendation The investigator (SC Coordinator or Principal) analyzes all evidence:

items: Evaluates credibility and reliability of information
Considers context and circumstances
Weighs mitigating and aggravating factors
Reviews student's prior record
Considers consistency with how similar cases have been handled
Prepares written summary of findings
Makes recommendation for classification and consequence

content.text: Step 7: Decision and Communication The appropriate authority makes the decision:

items: Level 1: Teacher (immediate, may be verbal)
Level 2: SC Coordinator (within 48 hours, in writing to parents)
Level 3: Principal (within 5 days, formal letter to parents)
Level 4: Discipline Committee/Board (within 10 days of Committee meeting)

content.text: The decision communication includes:

items: Summary of incident and findings
Classification level determined
Consequence(s) imposed
Rationale for decision
Any conditions or requirements
Support offered to student
Information about appeal rights (for Level 3-4)
Level 1: Addressed immediately or same day
Level 2: Investigation and response within 48 hours
Level 3: Investigation completed within 5 school days; decision communicated same day as completed

Level 4: Emergency measures within 24 hours; full investigation within 5 school days; Discipline Committee within 5 additional days; Yayasan Board (for expulsion) within 10 school days of Committee recommendation
content.title: ![][image2]22.4.4 Timeline Standards

content.text: Timelines may be extended in complex cases with notification to parents.
Misconduct during Cambridge examinations follows additional procedures: During Examination:

items: Invigilator records concern on Incident Report
Student allowed to continue exam (unless actively disrupting others)
Invigilator informs Examinations Officer immediately after exam
Student not informed of concern during exam (to avoid distress affecting performance)
content.title: 22.4.5 Special Procedures for Examination Misconduct

content.text: Post-Examination:

items: Examinations Officer investigates and prepares report
Student interviewed with opportunity to respond
Principal reviews and determines if malpractice occurred
If malpractice found, report submitted to Cambridge using Form 4 within required timeframe
Cambridge makes final determination on consequences for examination

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 10.7 for detailed Cambridge malpractice procedures and reporting requirements.
For situations requiring immediate action (danger to self or others, criminal activity, etc.):

steps: Ensure immediate safety of all involved
Separate student(s) from situation
Contact emergency services if needed (police, medical)
Notify Principal immediately (or most senior staff available)
Contact parents as soon as possible
Document all actions taken
Activate safeguarding procedures if applicable
Provide support to affected parties
Preserve evidence for investigation
Principal may implement immediate suspension pending investigation
content.title: 22.4.6 Emergency Procedures

content.text: Definition: Student serves suspension on campus in a supervised space separate from regular classes. The student continues academic work and reflects on behavior through guided activities. Duration: 1-3 days Authorization: Principal Procedures:

steps: Principal issues written notice to parents specifying dates and reason
Student reports to designated supervision area (not regular classroom)
Teachers provide work for student to complete
Student completes reflection activities as assigned by SC Coordinator or Counselor
Student may eat lunch separately from peers
Reintegration meeting held before return to regular classes
For boarding students, suspension served in designated area with tutor supervision
content.title: 22.5.1 In-School Suspension

content.text: Student Responsibilities During In-School Suspension:

items: Arrive on time and remain in designated area
Complete all assigned academic work
Complete reflection activities
Behave respectfully toward supervising staff
Not communicate with peers during suspension period

content.text: Definition: Student is removed from school for a specified period and may not participate in any school activities during the suspension. Duration: 1-10 days depending on severity Authorization:

items: 1-3 days: Principal
4+ days: Principal with GM/Foundation Representative notification
content.title: 22.5.2 Out-of-School Suspension

content.text: Procedures:

steps: Principal issues written notice to parents specifying dates, reason, and conditions for return
Parents must collect student from school (student not sent home alone)
Student may not come to school premises during suspension
Student may not attend school events or activities during suspension
Teachers provide work for student to complete at home
Return-to-school conference required with parent, student, Principal, and SC Coordinator
Reintegration plan developed and documented
Follow-up monitoring schedule established

content.text: For Boarding Students:

items: Student returns home for duration of suspension
If return home not possible, alternative supervised arrangement made
Parents responsible for supervision during suspension period

content.text: Definition: Permanent removal of a student from the school. Expulsion is the most severe consequence and is reserved for the most serious violations or after other interventions have failed. Authorization: Yayasan Board (final decision) based on Discipline Committee recommendation Expulsion Criteria: Expulsion may be considered when:

items: Student has committed a Level 4 violation of extreme severity
Student has received three warning letters without improvement
Student's continued presence poses unacceptable risk to others
Student has committed criminal acts affecting the school community
All reasonable interventions have been exhausted without success
The behavior is fundamentally incompatible with school membership
content.title: 22.5.3 Expulsion Process

content.text: Formal Process: Step 1: Principal Recommendation Principal prepares comprehensive written recommendation to Discipline Committee including:

items: Complete documentation of incident(s) leading to recommendation
Student's disciplinary history
Interventions attempted and outcomes
Evidence supporting recommendation
Impact on victim(s) and school community
Any mitigating factors considered

content.text: Step 2: Discipline Committee Review The Discipline Committee convenes within 5 school days of receiving recommendation. Committee composition:

items: Principal (Chair)
GM/Foundation Representative
School Counselor
Teacher Representative (not directly involved in incident)
SC Coordinator (presenting officer, non-voting)

content.text: The Committee reviews all documentation and may request additional information. Step 3: Hearing Student and parents are invited to appear before the Committee:

items: Written notice provided at least 3 school days before hearing
Notice includes specific allegations, evidence summary, and potential outcome
Family may submit written statement in advance
At hearing, family has opportunity to present their perspective and any supporting evidence
Family may ask questions about the evidence
Committee members may ask questions of family
Family may bring an advocate (not legal representation in formal capacity)

content.text: Step 4: Committee Deliberation Following the hearing, the Committee deliberates privately:

items: Considers all evidence and testimony
Determines whether expulsion is warranted
If not expulsion, determines alternative consequence
Prepares written recommendation to Yayasan Board (for expulsion) or written decision (for alternative)

content.text: Step 5: Yayasan Board Decision (for expulsion) For expulsion recommendations, the Yayasan Board:

items: Reviews Committee recommendation and supporting documentation
May request family to appear or submit additional statement
Makes final decision within 10 school days of Committee recommendation
Decision requires majority vote of Board members present
Decision is final (subject to appeal as outlined in Section 22.6)

content.text: Step 6: Communication Written decision is provided to family including:

items: Decision reached (expulsion or alternative)
Effective date
Rationale for decision
Practical information (collecting belongings, records transfer, etc.)
Appeal rights and process

content.text: Immediate Actions Following Expulsion Decision:

items: Student's access to school systems terminated
Student collects personal belongings (supervised, at agreed time)
Academic records prepared for transfer to new school
Refund calculations completed per school policy
Cambridge notified if student was registered for examinations
Confidential summary prepared for any requesting schools

content.text: Students expelled from Eduversal Partner Schools are generally not eligible for readmission. In exceptional circumstances (e.g., significant time has passed, clear evidence of rehabilitation), a family may petition for reconsideration:

items: Petition submitted in writing to GM/Foundation Representative
Must be at least one full academic year after expulsion
Includes evidence of changed circumstances/behavior
Yayasan Board decides whether to consider
If considered, full re-application process required
No guarantee of acceptance
content.title: 22.5.4 Readmission After Expulsion

content.text: Students and parents have the right to appeal disciplinary decisions for Level 3 and Level 4 consequences. Appeals must be based on one or more of the following grounds:

items: Procedural Error: The investigation or decision-making process did not follow established procedures, and this affected the outcome.
New Evidence: Significant new evidence has emerged that was not available during the original investigation and could materially affect the decision.
Disproportionate Consequence: The consequence imposed is disproportionate to the offense considering all circumstances and how similar cases have been handled.
Misinterpretation of Facts: The findings of fact are not supported by the evidence or there was a significant error in understanding what occurred.
content.title: 22.6.1 Right to Appeal

content.text: Appeals may not be based simply on disagreement with the decision or the hope of a more lenient outcome.
Step 1: Written Appeal Submission

items: Submit written appeal to GM/Foundation Representative
Deadline: Within 5 school days of receiving the disciplinary decision
Include: Specific grounds for appeal, supporting evidence or argument, requested outcome
Receipt of appeal acknowledged within 2 school days
content.title: 22.6.2 Appeal Procedures

content.text: Step 2: Review of Appeal The GM/Foundation Representative (or designee not involved in original decision):

items: Reviews original investigation documentation
Reviews appeal submission
May request additional information from either party
May interview relevant parties if necessary
Does not re-investigate but reviews whether process was fair and decision reasonable

content.text: Step 3: Appeal Decision Written response provided within 10 school days of appeal submission:

items: Appeal upheld (original decision overturned or modified)
Appeal partially upheld (consequence modified)
Appeal denied (original decision stands)
Includes rationale for decision

content.text: Step 4: Final Appeal (Expulsion Cases Only) For expulsion decisions, families may submit final appeal to Eduversal Central Management:

items: Deadline: Within 10 school days of GM appeal decision
Submitted in writing to Eduversal Central Office
Central Management reviews for significant procedural concerns
Decision provided within 15 school days
This decision is final
For suspension: Suspension generally proceeds during appeal unless urgency of appeal review warrants delay
For expulsion: Student typically remains suspended (not attending) during appeal process
If appeal succeeds: Suspension days may be removed from record; student reinstated for expulsion cases
content.title: 22.6.3 Effect of Appeal on Consequences

content.text: Restorative practices represent a philosophical shift from asking "What rule was broken and what punishment is deserved?" to asking "Who was harmed, what are their needs, and how can we make things right?" This approach recognizes that misconduct causes harm to relationships and community, and that true accountability involves repairing that harm. Restorative practices at Eduversal Partner Schools:

items: Build positive relationships proactively
Respond to conflict and wrongdoing by addressing harm
Involve all affected parties in finding solutions
Focus on the future (making things right) rather than only the past (assigning blame)
Develop students' social-emotional skills and sense of responsibility
Strengthen community bonds rather than simply punishing individuals
content.title: 22.7.1 Philosophy of Restorative Practice

content.text: Restorative practices complement rather than replace traditional discipline. For serious offenses, restorative processes may be used alongside formal consequences to address the relational harm while still maintaining clear boundaries.

Tier 1: Universal/Preventive Practices (All Students, All Settings) These practices build positive relationships and prevent conflict: Community Circles (Circles for Community Building): Regular classroom meetings (weekly in Primary, biweekly in Secondary) where students sit in a circle and share using a talking piece. Circles build relationships, give students voice, develop communication skills, and create shared agreements. Topics might include sharing about weekends, discussing class challenges, celebrating achievements, or exploring values. Affective Statements: Teachers use "I-statements" to express feelings about behavior impact rather than accusatory language. For example: "I feel concerned when assignments aren't submitted because I want to make sure you're learning" rather than "You're lazy and irresponsible." This models emotional literacy and invites dialogue rather than defensiveness. Restorative Questions: Simple questions used in everyday situations to help students think about impact:

items: What happened?
What were you thinking at the time?
Who has been affected by what happened?
How have they been affected?
What do you think you need to do to make things right?
content.title: 22.7.2 Continuum of Restorative Approaches

content.text: Fair Process: Involving students in decisions that affect them (classroom rules, seating, activity choices) so they feel ownership and investment. Explaining the reasons behind rules and decisions. Tier 2: Responsive Practices (Targeted Intervention for Minor-Moderate Conflicts) These practices respond to conflicts and harm: Restorative Conversations: A one-on-one or small group dialogue between the person who caused harm and the person harmed, facilitated by a trained staff member. Used for minor conflicts, misunderstandings, and relationship repair. Duration: 15-30 minutes. Structure:

steps: Each person shares their perspective (using talking piece)
Facilitator helps identify the harm caused
Person who caused harm acknowledges impact
Discussion of how to make things right
Agreement reached and follow-up planned

content.text: Peer Mediation: Trained student mediators help peers resolve conflicts through structured dialogue. Appropriate for peer disputes where both parties are willing to participate. Student mediators are trained and supervised by school staff. Peer Mediation Process:

steps: Mediators explain ground rules (respect, honesty, confidentiality)
Each party shares their perspective without interruption
Parties identify common ground and differences
Brainstorm solutions together
Reach agreement and document
Follow-up scheduled

content.text: Small Group Conferences: A facilitated meeting involving the student who caused harm, those affected, and relevant supporters (teachers, parents). Used for more significant incidents affecting multiple people. Duration: 45-60 minutes. Tier 3: Intensive Practices (Serious Incidents Requiring Formal Process) Formal Restorative Conferences: A structured conference for serious incidents, involving:

items: Student who caused harm and their parent(s)
Victim(s) and their parent(s) (if willing to participate)
Affected community members (teachers, peers who witnessed, etc.)
Trained facilitator (Counselor or external specialist)
Principal or SC Coordinator as observer

content.text: Conference Process:

steps: Facilitator explains process and ground rules (90-120 minutes)
Person who caused harm describes what happened and their thinking
Each affected person describes how they were impacted
Parents/supporters share their perspectives
Person who caused harm responds to what they've heard
Group discusses what needs to happen to make things right
Written agreement developed with specific commitments
Follow-up plan established

content.text: Participation is voluntary for victims; they may choose to participate directly, send a written statement, have a representative speak for them, or not participate. The conference can still proceed without victim participation, focusing on the responsible student's accountability. Reintegration Circles: Support a student's return to class or school after suspension or extended absence due to discipline matter. The circle welcomes the student back, allows them to share their commitments, and allows the community to express what they need from the student going forward.

Agreements developed through restorative processes may include: Acknowledgment: Written or verbal acknowledgment of what happened and acceptance of responsibility. This is not about shame but about honest accountability. Apology: Sincere apology to those harmed—may be verbal, written, or both. A good apology acknowledges specific harm, expresses genuine remorse, commits to change, and asks what else is needed. Restitution: Practical repair for material harm—payment for damaged items, replacement of stolen property, repair of damage caused. Restitution may be financial or through service. Service Contribution: Community service that benefits those harmed or the broader community. Should be meaningful and connected to the harm where possible (e.g., helping maintain areas that were vandalized). Behavioral Commitments: Specific, measurable commitments about future behavior. Should be realistic and achievable. Support Needed: What support the student needs to succeed (counseling, academic help, check-ins with mentor, etc.). Follow-up Plan: Timeline for checking on agreement completion, who will monitor, and what happens if agreement is not fulfilled. All agreements are documented in writing, signed by participants, and filed in EduNav. Agreement completion is tracked and reported.
content.title: 22.7.3 Restorative Agreement Components

content.text: Staff receive training to implement restorative approaches:

items: All Teachers: 6-hour introduction during professional development days; focus on affective statements, restorative questions, and classroom circles
Homeroom Teachers: Additional 3-hour training on leading community circles and facilitating restorative conversations
SC Coordinator and Counselor: 2-day intensive training on facilitating formal restorative conferences; annual refresher training
Student Peer Mediators: 8-hour training program covering mediation skills, confidentiality, when to refer; ongoing supervision
content.title: 22.7.4 Training for Restorative Practice

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 12.4 for overall professional development framework and DTP requirements.

Definition and Scope: Eduversal Partner Schools strictly prohibit bullying in all forms. Bullying is defined as repeated, intentional behavior that causes physical, emotional, or psychological harm and typically involves a real or perceived power imbalance between the bully and target. A single severe incident may also constitute bullying. Bullying is prohibited:

items: On school premises
During school-related activities
On school transportation
Online when affecting school community members (cyberbullying)
Off-campus when it affects the school environment or community
content.title: 22.8.1 Anti-Bullying Policy

content.text: Types of Bullying: Physical Bullying: Hitting, kicking, pushing, tripping, spitting, damaging property, unwanted physical contact, intimidating gestures or blocking movement. Verbal Bullying: Name-calling, insults, teasing, threats, intimidation, offensive comments about appearance/ability/background, spreading rumors. Social/Relational Bullying: Deliberate exclusion, manipulating friendships, public humiliation, spreading rumors, damaging reputation, isolating individuals. Cyberbullying: Harassment via technology including offensive messages, sharing embarrassing images/videos without consent, impersonation online, excluding from online groups, sharing private information, creating fake accounts, online threats. Response: Bullying is treated as a minimum Level 3 infraction. Severe or repeated bullying may constitute Level 4. Response includes:

items: Immediate intervention to stop the bullying
Support for the target (counseling, safety planning, monitoring)
Investigation following Level 3-4 procedures
Consequence for the bully appropriate to severity
Restorative conference if target is willing
Follow-up monitoring to ensure bullying does not continue
Parent involvement for both parties

content.text: Prevention: Schools implement anti-bullying programs including:

items: Education about bullying in assemblies and PSHE curriculum
Student voice mechanisms for reporting concerns
Training for staff on recognizing and responding to bullying
Clear communication that bullying is unacceptable
Positive bystander training for students
Monitoring of high-risk locations and times
Regular climate surveys to assess prevalence

content.text: Reporting: Students can report bullying through:

items: Telling any trusted adult (teacher, counselor, staff)
Anonymous reporting through suggestion box or online form
Speaking to SC Coordinator or Principal directly
Parents contacting school on child's behalf

content.text: All reports are taken seriously and investigated. Students will not face negative consequences for good-faith reporting. Cross-Reference: See Section 9.6 for comprehensive anti-bullying procedures within safeguarding framework; see Section 21.4 for cyberbullying specific to digital environment.

Statement: Academic integrity is fundamental to the educational mission of Eduversal Partner Schools. Students are expected to complete their own work honestly, acknowledge sources appropriately, and maintain integrity in all assessments. Academic dishonesty undermines learning and violates Cambridge examination regulations. Prohibited Behaviors: Plagiarism: Presenting others' work, ideas, or words as one's own without proper acknowledgment. This includes copying from sources (including internet) without citation, submitting purchased or downloaded work, and paraphrasing too closely without credit. Collusion: Unauthorized collaboration on work that is supposed to be completed individually. While collaboration is encouraged when permitted, submitting collaborative work as individual work is collusion. Cheating: Using unauthorized materials or assistance during assessments—notes, electronic devices, communication with others, copying from another student's work, obtaining advance information about assessment content. Fabrication: Inventing or falsifying data, sources, citations, or experimental results. This includes citing sources not actually consulted and inventing quotations. Facilitating Dishonesty: Helping others commit academic dishonesty—sharing work for copying, providing unauthorized assistance during assessments, allowing others to copy. AI Misuse: Using artificial intelligence tools to generate work without authorization or acknowledgment. When AI use is permitted, students must disclose its use and the nature of assistance received. Teaching Academic Integrity: Schools proactively teach academic integrity:

items: Age-appropriate instruction on citation and referencing
Clear explanation of what constitutes dishonesty
Practice with paraphrasing and summarizing
Discussion of why integrity matters
Guidance on appropriate use of AI tools
Academic integrity agreement signed by students
content.title: 22.8.2 Academic Integrity Policy

content.text: Consequences: First Offense (Minor): Level 2 response—zero on the specific assignment, educational conversation about integrity, opportunity to redo for reduced credit, parent notification. Focus on teaching rather than punishment. Repeated Offenses or Serious First Offense: Level 3 response—warning letter, zero on assignment without redo opportunity, possible course grade penalty, academic integrity contract. Examination Misconduct: Reported to Cambridge Assessment International Education per Centre requirements. Cambridge determines consequences which may include disqualification from subject or all subjects. For Cambridge Examinations: Any suspected malpractice during Cambridge examinations must be:

items: Documented on incident report during/after exam
Reported to Principal and Examinations Officer immediately
Investigated promptly
Reported to Cambridge using Form 4 if malpractice is suspected
Subject to Cambridge determination of consequences

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 6.4 for academic integrity within assessment policy; Section 10.7 for Cambridge malpractice reporting procedures.

Acceptable Use: Students must adhere to the Acceptable Use Policy (AUP) for all school technology and personal devices used for school purposes. The AUP is signed by students and parents at enrollment and annually thereafter. Mobile Phone Policy: Mobile phones present both opportunities and risks in educational settings. Schools implement the following framework: General Rules:

items: Mobile phones must be collected by homeroom teachers at the start of the school day (day schools) or by dormitory tutors (boarding schools)
Phones are stored securely and returned at end of day/designated times
Unauthorized use during school hours is prohibited
School may inspect device contents if there is reasonable suspicion of policy violation
content.title: 22.8.3 Technology and Digital Citizenship Policy

content.text: Boarding School Schedule (Example):

items: Saturday: 05:00-22:00
Other times: Permission required with recommendation from teacher/tutor

content.text: Consequences for Unauthorized Use:

items: First offense: Confiscation for one month
Second offense: Confiscation for one semester
Third offense: Permanent confiscation (device may be auctioned with proceeds to charity)

content.text: Parents may collect confiscated devices during the confiscation period with signed letter from parents. Students may be required to provide unlock codes if device is suspected of containing policy-violating content. AI Tools: Artificial intelligence tools may only be used within teacher-approved guidelines. Unauthorized or undisclosed AI use in assessments constitutes academic dishonesty. When AI use is permitted, students must:

items: Only use approved AI tools
Disclose AI assistance in their work
Maintain original thinking and personal voice
Not submit AI-generated content as their own work

content.text: Online Conduct: Students must maintain respectful, appropriate behavior in all online interactions related to school:

items: No cyberbullying, harassment, or threats
No sharing inappropriate content
Respect privacy of others (no sharing photos without consent)
Represent school appropriately on social media
Report concerns about online safety

content.text: Cross-Reference: See Section 21 for comprehensive Digital Learning and Technology policy including full AUP.

Authority: The school has the right to conduct searches of student belongings to maintain a safe environment. Students should not have expectation of privacy for items brought onto school premises when there is reasonable cause for search. Types of Searches: Routine/Random Searches:

items: Conducted at least monthly
Supervised by Principal and/or Head of Boarding
Search team includes SC Coordinator, academic coordinator, dormitory staff, and picket teachers
All students/rooms in selected area searched equally
Items searched: bags, pockets, lockers, dormitory rooms
Focus: electronic devices, prohibited items
content.title: 22.8.4 Search and Seizure Policy

content.text: Individual Searches:

items: Require reasonable suspicion of policy violation
Authorized by Principal
Conducted by same-gender staff member with witness present
Student informed of reason for search
Parent notified afterward (unless doing so would compromise investigation)

content.text: Search Procedure:

steps: Search plan known only to management team (prevents items being hidden)
Search team equips with label paper, collection bags, documentation forms
Students asked to stand away from belongings
Belongings searched systematically
Found items labeled with ownership and documented
Items taken to office for inspection
Owners of confiscated items notified and consequences applied per policy

content.text: Prohibited Items: Items that may not be brought to school include:

items: Weapons (knives, sharp objects, items that could cause harm)
Drugs, alcohol, tobacco, vaping devices
Pornographic or inappropriate materials
Gambling equipment
Non-prescribed medications (must be held by clinic)
Items violating dress code
Unauthorized electronics during restricted times

content.text: Handling of Items:

items: Dangerous/illegal items: Reported to authorities; not returned
Prohibited but not illegal items: Confiscated per relevant policy; may be returned to parents
Items held as evidence: Retained until investigation complete
All confiscations documented

content.text: Prohibition: The possession, use, distribution, or being under the influence of alcohol, illegal drugs, tobacco, vaping products, or non-prescribed controlled substances is strictly prohibited on school premises, at school events, on school transportation, and for boarding students, in dormitories. Response: Substance abuse violations are typically Level 4 infractions:

items: Immediate removal from class/activity
Parent notification
Possible police involvement for illegal substances
Mandatory counseling/intervention program
Suspension (length depends on circumstances)
Distribution: likely expulsion
First-time possession/use: serious consequences but may not be expulsion if student engages with intervention
content.title: 22.8.5 Substance Abuse Policy

content.text: Support: Students who self-disclose substance concerns before any incident may receive support rather than punishment:

items: Counseling and intervention services
Referral to appropriate professionals
Confidentiality maintained to extent possible
Monitoring and check-ins

content.text: The school aims to balance safety of community with support for students struggling with substance issues.

For Islamic Boarding Schools (Pesantren) and other residential programs, the Code of Conduct applies 24/7 throughout the student's residence. Students represent the school at all times while in boarding.
content.title: 22.9.1 Extended Scope of Conduct Expectations

content.text: Additional expectations for boarding students include: Daily Schedule Compliance:

items: Adherence to prayer times (fajr, dhuhr, asr, maghrib, isha for Islamic boarding)
Participation in mandatory study periods
Observance of lights-out and wake-up times
Attendance at meals during designated times
Participation in dormitory activities and programs
content.title: 22.9.2 Dormitory-Specific Expectations

content.text: Room and Living Standards:

items: Maintaining cleanliness of personal space and shared areas
Respecting roommates' belongings and space
Following room inspection standards
Proper care of school-provided furnishings
Not bringing prohibited items into dormitory

content.text: Gender Interaction:

items: Strict adherence to gender-appropriate areas
Following guidelines for interaction between genders
No entering dormitory areas of opposite gender
Appropriate conduct in co-educational spaces

content.text: Leave and Movement:

items: Proper authorization for leaving campus (written permission from parents on file)
Sign-out/sign-in procedures for all departures
Reporting whereabouts at all times
Not leaving dormitory after curfew without tutor permission
Weekend home leave per approved schedule

content.text: Self-Study Period Rules:

items: Remaining in designated study areas during self-study time
No accessing online shopping or non-educational sites
No eating in study rooms (heavy meals)
No music through speakers during study time
Not leaving study room without tutor permission
Digital devices for learning only

content.text: Electronic Devices:

items: Mobile phones collected and secured by dormitory tutors
Use only during designated times (typically weekends)
Recommendation letter required for use outside scheduled times
School reserves right to inspect device contents
Progressive confiscation policy for violations

content.text: Handling Authority:

items: Minor issues (Level 1): Dormitory Tutor addresses directly
Moderate issues (Level 2): Dormitory Director coordinates with SC Coordinator
Serious issues (Level 3-4): Principal handles with dormitory leadership involvement
content.title: 22.9.3 Dormitory Discipline Protocols

content.text: Dormitory-Specific Consequences: In addition to standard consequences, boarding students may face:

items: Extra cleaning or dormitory duties
Loss of weekend home leave privileges
Restriction to dormitory during free time
Earlier curfew or extended study hours
Loss of electronic device privileges
Temporary room reassignment
Loss of boarding privileges (for severe/repeated violations) before expulsion

content.text: Weekend Leave Restrictions:

items: Level 2 offenses: May lose one weekend leave
Level 3 offenses: May lose multiple weekend leaves
Repeated violations: Extended restriction from home leave

content.text: Communication with Home:

items: Parents informed of all significant behavioral concerns
Regular update calls or messages from dormitory staff
Parent conferences may be conducted by video call if in-person not possible

content.text: The extended time with boarding students allows for strong positive reinforcement:

items: Recognition points for positive behaviors (punctuality, helping others, academic effort)
Accumulated points toward privileges or awards
Weekly/monthly recognition for exemplary students
Leadership opportunities for consistent positive conduct
Communication to parents about positive developments
content.title: 22.9.4 Positive Recognition for Boarding Students

content.text: All behavioral incidents and interventions must be documented in EduNav to ensure consistent tracking, identify patterns, and inform improvement: Incident Reports:

items: Complete description of incident (who, what, when, where)
Names of witnesses
Evidence collected
Initial response taken
Classification level assigned
Follow-up actions planned
content.title: 22.10.1 Documentation Requirements

content.text: Conference Notes:

items: Date, attendees, purpose of meeting
Key points discussed
Agreements reached
Action items and responsibilities
Follow-up scheduled

content.text: Warning Letters:

items: Formal letter with incident summary and expectations
Parent acknowledgment signature
Filed in student confidential file
Copy in EduNav

content.text: Intervention Records:

items: Behavioral intervention plans
Progress monitoring data
Support provided (counseling, mentoring, etc.)
Modifications made

content.text: Restorative Conference Records:

items: Participants and roles
Summary of conference process
Agreement reached (specific commitments)
Follow-up plan
Completion tracking

content.text: Monthly Analysis: SC Coordinator reviews behavioral data monthly to identify:

items: Incident frequency and trends
Patterns by time, location, student cohort
Effectiveness of interventions
Students needing additional support
Environmental factors to address
Staff development needs
content.title: 22.10.2 Data Analysis and Reporting

content.text: Report submitted to Principal with recommendations. Term Reports: Behavior data included in school improvement reporting:

items: Total incidents by level
Disaggregation by grade, gender, type
Comparison to previous terms/years
Suspension and expulsion data
Restorative conference completion rates
Progress on behavioral KPIs

content.text: Shared with GM/Foundation Representative and incorporated into School Improvement Plan review. Annual Review: Comprehensive analysis informing policy updates:

items: Full year data analysis and trends
Stakeholder feedback (staff, students, parents)
Effectiveness of current approaches
Recommendations for policy modifications
Professional development priorities for coming year
Resource needs identified

content.text: Schools monitor the following KPIs for student conduct: Incident Rate:

items: Target: Disciplinary incident rate below 5% of student population per term
Calculation: (Students with Level 2+ incidents ÷ Total enrollment) × 100
Tracked monthly and reported termly
content.title: 22.10.3 Key Performance Indicators

content.text: Restorative Agreement Completion:

items: Target: 80%+ successful completion of restorative agreements
Calculation: (Agreements fully completed ÷ Total agreements made) × 100
Tracked per agreement; reported termly

content.text: Repeat Offense Reduction:

items: Target: Reduce repeat offenses by 10% annually
Calculation: Compare students with multiple incidents year-over-year
Reported annually

content.text: Student Wellbeing Survey:

items: Target: Student satisfaction with school climate above 85%
Questions include feeling safe, feeling respected, fairness of rules
Administered annually; results inform improvement

content.text: Staff Confidence:

items: Target: 90%+ staff confident in managing behavior
Survey on behavior management confidence and support
Administered annually

content.text: Behavioral records are confidential:

items: Access limited to Principal, SC Coordinator, homeroom teacher, and staff with legitimate need
Parents may request to review their child's behavioral record
Records secured in EduNav with appropriate access controls
Physical files locked and secure
Records retained per school retention policy (typically 7 years after departure)
Destruction of records follows secure protocols
Records may be shared with authorities when legally required
content.title: 22.10.4 Confidentiality of Records

items: Overall accountability for student conduct and discipline implementation
Final authority for Level 3 decisions; participates in Level 4 decisions
Chairs Discipline Committee
Ensures adequate resources for behavior management
Monitors behavioral data and adjusts strategies
Reports to Yayasan Board on serious matters and trends
Models expected behavior and sets school tone
Communicates expectations to community
content.title: 22.11.1 Principal

items: Oversight of discipline policy implementation across school
Receives notification of suspensions exceeding 3 days
Hears appeals of Principal decisions
Participates in Discipline Committee for Level 4 cases
Liaison with Yayasan Board for expulsion decisions
Monitors compliance with Eduversal standards
content.title: 22.11.2 General Manager/Foundation Representative

items: Day-to-day behavior management leadership
Investigates Level 2 and assists with Level 3-4 incidents
Prepares warning letters and documentation
Maintains all behavior records in EduNav
Coordinates restorative conferences and interventions
Trains staff on behavior management and restorative practices
Monitors behavioral patterns and reports to Principal
Coordinates with Counselor on student support
Liaisons with parents on behavioral matters
Supervises peer mediation program
Manages search and seizure procedures
content.title: 22.11.3 School Culture (SC) Coordinator

items: First point of contact for student behavior concerns
Implements classroom-level interventions (Level 1)
Communicates with parents about behavioral concerns and progress
Participates in conferences for homeroom students
Supports implementation of behavior plans
Monitors homeroom students' overall wellbeing and conduct
Develops class agreements with students
Builds positive relationships that prevent behavior issues
content.title: 22.11.4 Homeroom Teacher

items: Manage Level 1 behaviors within their classrooms
Report incidents to SC Coordinator as required
Implement restorative practices in classroom (circles, affective statements)
Model expected behaviors and maintain positive classroom culture
Contribute to behavior plans for students in their classes
Document patterns of concern
Communicate with homeroom teachers about concerns
content.title: 22.11.5 Subject Teachers

items: Provides social-emotional learning support to students
Facilitates restorative conferences (Tier 2-3)
Develops behavioral intervention plans for students with chronic concerns
Supports students returning from suspension (reintegration)
Provides individual counseling for students with behavioral challenges
Advises SC Coordinator and Principal on student wellbeing factors
Trains students in peer mediation
Connects families with external support resources when needed
content.title: 22.11.6 Counselor

items: Dormitory Director: Coordinates behavior management in residential setting; works with SC Coordinator on Level 2+ issues; manages tutor team
Dormitory Tutors: Day-to-day supervision; address Level 1 issues; monitor compliance with dormitory expectations; build positive relationships with students; communicate with parents
content.title: 22.11.7 Dormitory Staff (Boarding Schools)

items: Follow Code of Conduct and classroom agreements
Report safety or wellbeing concerns (their own or others')
Participate constructively in peer mediation (trained students)
Take responsibility for own actions and choices
Engage genuinely in restorative processes
Support positive school culture
Respect rights and dignity of all community members
content.title: 22.11.8 Students

items: Reinforce school behavioral expectations at home
Respond promptly to school communications about behavior
Participate in conferences when requested
Support implementation of consequences and agreements
Partner with school in behavior improvement plans
Inform school of circumstances affecting student behavior
Model respectful engagement with school community
content.title: 22.11.9 Parents/Guardians

content.text: Staff receive training to implement this policy effectively: Annual Policy Refresher: All staff review the discipline policy and procedures at beginning of year professional development days. Includes updates, case studies, and Q\&A. Restorative Practices Training: Mandatory for all teachers; covers philosophy, affective statements, restorative questions, and classroom circles (6 hours). Advanced training for SC Coordinator and Counselor on facilitating formal conferences (16+ hours). Annual refresher training. Classroom Management: Incorporated into DTP (Development of Teaching Proficiency) program modules on managing learning environments. New teacher induction includes specific training on behavior expectations and procedures. Cultural Sensitivity: Training on diverse cultural backgrounds, communication styles, and EAL considerations in behavior management. Understanding how cultural factors may affect student behavior and adult responses. De-escalation Techniques: Practical training for managing challenging situations safely—recognizing escalation, calming techniques, when to seek help, physical safety considerations. Trauma-Informed Practice: Awareness training on how trauma affects student behavior and how to respond supportively while maintaining expectations. Peer Mediation Training: SC Coordinator and Counselor trained to deliver student peer mediator training program. Cross-Reference: See Section 12.4 for DTP professional development framework.
This policy is reviewed annually: Timeline:

items: April-May: Gather feedback from stakeholders
June: Analyze behavioral data for year
July: Policy review committee meets
August: Revisions finalized and approved
September: Updated policy communicated and implemented
content.title: 22.13.1 Annual Review Process

content.text: Review Committee:

items: Principal (chair)
SC Coordinator
Counselor
Teacher representatives (Primary, Secondary)
Student representatives (Secondary)
Parent representative

content.text: Data Analysis:

items: Incident data by level, type, cohort
Trend analysis over multiple years
Effectiveness of interventions
KPI performance
content.title: 22.13.2 Inputs to Review

content.text: Stakeholder Feedback:

items: Staff survey on policy clarity and effectiveness
Student survey on fairness and understanding
Parent survey on communication and satisfaction
Focus groups as needed

content.text: External Input:

items: Changes to Indonesian regulations
Updates to Cambridge requirements
CIS/WASC standards updates
Best practice research

content.text: All updates documented with:

items: Version number and date
Summary of changes
Approval signatures (Principal, GM)
Effective date
Communication plan
content.title: 22.13.3 Version Control

content.text: Previous versions retained for reference.

subsections.title: Key Evidence and Artifacts for Accreditation

content.text: Schools maintain the following documentation for CIS/WASC accreditation visits:

steps: Student Code of Conduct (bilingual versions with parent acknowledgment records)
Incident logs and behavior data reports from EduNav (anonymized for review)
Sample investigation files demonstrating due process
Restorative conference records and agreement completion rates
Discipline Committee meeting minutes (redacted as appropriate)
Warning letter templates and sample letters (anonymized)
Staff training records for behavior management and restorative practices
Student and parent survey results on school climate and fairness
Annual behavior data analysis reports with KPI tracking
Peer mediation program documentation (training, logs, outcomes)
Policy review records showing annual evaluation
Professional development materials on behavior management
Classroom agreements samples
Parent communication samples related to behavior

content.text: CIS/WASC Standards:

items: Domain C: Student Wellbeing—ensuring safe, supportive environment
Domain E: Student Support Systems—behavior management and intervention

content.text: Indonesian Regulations:

items: UU No. 35/2014 concerning Child Protection—fair treatment, protection from violence
Permendikbud provisions on student rights and discipline in schools
Ministry guidelines on student welfare and school safety

content.text: Cambridge International:

items: Candidate conduct rules for examination candidates
Malpractice reporting requirements and procedures
Centre expectations for maintaining integrity

content.text: Eduversal QMS:

items: School Culture KPIs in School Appraisal criteria
EduNav behavior tracking and reporting
EASE evaluation indicators for student wellbeing
Teacher KPI on classroom management
